topic: reflecting on the year — from villas to museums, from pilates to muay thai

turns.content: do you ever just sit down and realize how different your life is compared to a year ago?? because I'm having one of those moments rn and it's kind of hitting me
Those moments of sudden perspective can be really powerful. What's bringing this on — did something specific trigger it, or did it just hit you?

I was scrolling through my photos from last May and literally everything is different. the projects I work on, what I do for exercise, where my friends are, what I want from my career. like if you showed May 2025 Zara a snapshot of my life right now she would not believe it

That's a big realization. A year is such a weird unit of time — it feels short when you're living it day by day, but when you zoom out like this, the cumulative change can be staggering. Is the feeling more exciting or more disorienting? Or some mix?

tbh both?? like on one hand I'm really proud of where I am now. I went from working on residential stuff to this incredible project that actually challenges me creatively. I switched from pilates to muay thai which sounds random but it changed my whole energy honestly. but on the other hand it's like... am I even the same person?? that sounds dramatic I know lol

It doesn't sound dramatic at all. When enough things shift in a short period, it's natural to feel unmoored from your previous sense of self. The person who was doing pilates and residential villas had a certain identity and rhythm, and the person doing muay thai on a creatively demanding project is operating differently. Both are you, but the texture of daily life is really different.

The muay thai switch is interesting to me — what made you make that change?

I think I needed something that let me be aggressive?? like pilates was great for my body and my posture but it was very controlled and quiet and I realized I was being controlled and quiet in every area of my life. muay thai is loud and messy and you get hit and you hit back and idk it woke something up in me. I come out of class drenched in sweat feeling like I could fight god and that energy carries into my work

"Drenched in sweat feeling like I could fight god" — that's an incredible description and it tells me the switch wasn't really about exercise at all. It was about permission. Permission to be intense, to take up space, to not be quiet and composed all the time.

And the fact that it carries into your work makes total sense. Creative work requires a certain amount of aggression — the willingness to commit to a bold idea, to defend your design decisions, to push back when someone wants to water things down. Pilates Zara might have deferred. Muay thai Zara throws the punch.

Do you think the project change and the exercise change are connected, or did they just happen to coincide?

wallah I never thought about it that way but you might be right. they happened around the same time and I think the project pushed me to level up in ways I wasn't expecting and muay thai was like... the physical manifestation of that shift?? like my body needed to match where my brain was going. ok this is getting deep lol

It IS deep but in a good way — these are genuine insights about yourself, not just navel-gazing. The body-brain alignment thing is real. When you're intellectually challenged and growing, your body wants to match that intensity. A gentle stretching routine starts to feel mismatched with who you're becoming.

What about the other changes you mentioned — friends being in different places, career ambitions shifting? Do those feel like part of the same wave or separate?

same wave I think. my best friend moved abroad and that was hard but it also forced me to like... stand on my own more?? I used to rely on her energy so much. now I have to bring my own energy to things. and career wise I've let go of some plans I had that weren't really mine, they were just what I thought I should want. so it all feels connected. like one thing shifts and everything else has to recalibrate

"Plans that weren't really mine" — that's one of the most important things a person in their twenties can recognize. There's a whole category of ambitions that are really just inherited expectations or prestige scripts, and letting go of them feels like failure until you realize it's actually clarity.

And the friendship shift — learning to bring your own energy instead of relying on someone else's — that's a form of growing up that doesn't get talked about enough. It doesn't mean the friendship is less, it means *you're* more.

Here's what I notice from everything you're describing: every change moved you toward more autonomy and more authenticity. The project that challenges you instead of the safe one. The exercise that matches your intensity. The career path that's actually yours. The independence that comes from your person being far away. That's a really coherent direction even if it felt chaotic while it was happening.

ok stop I'm gonna cry in this coffee shop. ngl I needed to hear that because from the inside it just felt like everything was falling apart and rearranging and I couldn't tell if I was growing or just losing things. but you're right it does have a direction. I'm more ME now than I was a year ago even though I can't fully describe who that is yet

"More me even though I can't fully describe who that is yet" — honestly, that's the most authentic place to be at 23. Anyone who claims to have themselves fully figured out at your age is either lying or not paying attention. You're supposed to be in motion right now. The fact that you can feel the direction without being able to name the destination is not confusion — it's growth happening in real time.

And you're not losing things. You're trading things that fit the old version for things that fit the current one. That always feels like loss in the moment because the old things were comfortable and familiar. But comfort and growth don't usually live in the same place.

Don't cry in the coffee shop though, finish your drink with dignity and then cry in your car like a normal person.

LMAOOO too late there are tears in my latte. but honestly thank you this was the most unexpectedly therapeutic conversation. I came in here to scroll my phone and ended up having a whole existential moment. I think I just needed someone to tell me the changes are adding up to something good because from the inside you can't always see it

Sometimes you need an outside perspective to see the pattern in what feels like chaos. And for what it's worth — from everything you've described, the pattern is clear. You're building a version of your life that actually matches who you are, not who you thought you were supposed to be. That's brave work even when it doesn't feel like it.

Now go wipe your eyes, finish your latte, and enjoy the fact that May 2026 Zara is someone May 2025 Zara would be genuinely impressed by. You earned this version. Yalla, go live it.
